text: The INOR-8 tube bundle of the primary heat exchanger of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment contains 163 1/2-in. diam × 0.042-in.-wall U-tubes welded to a 1 1/2-in.-thick tube sheet, 17 in. in diameter.
Procedures were successfully developed for welding and back brazing the closely spaced 326 tube-to-tube sheet joints in the unit, and the actual tube bundle was fabricated without incident.
The 82 Au-18 Ni (wt %) brazing alloy used in this application is corrosion resistant, ductile, and produces joints exhibiting satisfactory mechanical strength.
The completed unit passed both helium-leak and 800-psi hydrostatic tests.
A nonfuel-bearing coolant salt, LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub>, circulates through the tube side of the heat exchanger and an air-cooled radiator.
The containment material is the commercially available alloy, INOR-8 (Ni-17 Mo-7 Cr-4 Fe, wt %).
The heat exchanger is of the conventional U-tube design with the tubes being 0.5-in.
All tube ends are joined to a 1 l/2-in.-thick INOR-8 tube sheet, 17 in. in diameter, as shown in Fig.
Design data for the unit are presented in Table 1.

However, as has been reported, $^{4}$ joints of this type, although initially sound, are subject to cracking during cyclic service at high temperatures.
The desired features of the welds were: (1) a minimum of l-T penetration $(\mathrm{T} = \text{tube wall thickness of 0.042 in.})$ , (2) no surface imperfections, (3) no porosity, and (4) no cracks.
This was obtained by flaring the joint on the weld side of the tube sheet to a maximum depth of 1/8 in.
The procedure was as follows: (1) the tube sheet part of the joint was flared to a 0.530-in.
ID with a special punch and (2) the tube end was extended through the tube sheet and flared to a 0.532-in.
With a 0.002-in. interference fit, the tube end had to be tapped level with the tube sheet surface for welding.
The welding parameters (i.e., current, travel speed, arc distance, etc.) were adjusted to provide the desired 1-T minimum penetration without excessive roll-over.
A travel speed of 7.3 in./min was found to provide consistently sound welds and allow satisfactory operator control.
The welding conditions finally selected for use on the MSRE heat exchanger are listed in Table 2.

In addition to the strength data, long-time diffusion studies of INOR-8 lap joints brazed with the gold-nickel alloy have also been conducted.
Specimens were aged for 1000, 3000, 5000, 7000, and 10,000 hr at both 1200 and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in air.
5), there was no detectable hardness difference between the diffusion zone and the unaffected base metal.
Also, no detectable hardness change was observed in the base metal, the diffusion zone, or the braze after aging at either 1200 or $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Thus, aging at these temperatures for up to 10,000 hr would be expected to have an insignificant effect on the joint strength and overall base-metal properties.
2 it can be seen that there must be enough brazing alloy to (1) fill the joint, (2) fill the three feeder holes, and (3) form a fillet at the junction of the tube and tube sheet face.
The results of these calculations are listed in Table 4.

Only a slight change in the appearance of the diffusion zone is evident after aging at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a definite boundary for the diffusion zone is not apparent and a general carbide precipitate is visible in the INOR-8.
Wire preforms, 3/32 in. in diameter, were found to provide an adequate supply of alloy, and the necessary size of trepan could be machined around each joint without encroaching on a neighbor.
Consequently, one 3/32-in. split ring was positioned in each trepan groove before assembly with the tubes.
6 were assembled, welded, and back brazed.
Specimens were heated to the brazing temperature of $1835^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at three different rates of temperature rise, 135, 270, and $405^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ .
In addition, another specimen was brazed at $1785^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

This specimen revealed incomplete filleting and joint penetration; thus it was apparent that temperature gradients over the actual unit should be controlled to ensure that the lowest temperature is always above $1835^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A proof test of all welding, brazing, and assembly conditions and techniques was carried out on a subsize 12-in.-diam assembly containing 98 joints with some short U-tubes.

Sufficient material in the form of INOR-8 tube sheet forgings and tubes was available for only one heat exchanger core, and replacement would have meant about a one-year delay and a large financial expenditure.
8, contained nine full-length U-tubes and 54 welded and back-brazed joints.
These joints were positioned in the center of the tube sheet and at 3, 6, 9, and 12 o'clock positions around the periphery.

section: FABRICATION OF MSRE HEAT EXCHANGER TUBE BUNDLE
text: The rate of temperature rise was approximately $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ up to an equalization temperature of $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ where it was held until all thermocouples were within $\pm 25^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: FABRICATION OF MSRE HEAT EXCHANGER TUBE BUNDLE
text: The initial part of the cycle was characterized by large thermal gradients between the center and edge of the tube sheet (approximately $150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) and between the heavy tube sheet and the top of the U-tubes (approximately $450^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) despite the fact that heat was applied in the pit section of the furnace only.

section: FABRICATION OF MSRE HEAT EXCHANGER TUBE BUNDLE
text: As the cycle progressed, regulation of the upper and lower zones of the gas-fired furnace reduced these gradients to about $50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ approaching the $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hold.

section: FABRICATION OF MSRE HEAT EXCHANGER TUBE BUNDLE
text: After 20 min at $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the temperature was increased to the brazing range of $1850 - 1885^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and held for 1 hr.

section: FABRICATION OF MSRE HEAT EXCHANGER TUBE BUNDLE
text: A slow furnace cool at about $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ was maintained to below $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ where an exothermic gas purge was introduced.
16, these being typical of all visible joints.
The brazed joints were then inspected by a newly developed ultrasonic technique.[9]

With the tubes filled with water, the ultrasonic probe, which contains a sending and receiving crystal mounted at a preset angle to each other, was rotated 360 deg around the inside of the tube and indexed down the tube in steps to fully cover the brazed area.
Figure 18 shows the completed tube bundle mounted on the fixture.
After welding the tube bundle into its shell, helium leak and 800-psi hydrostatic tests were conducted.
19 ready for installation in the MSR system.

The results of these investigations culminated in the fabrication of a heat exchanger tube bundle which required no repairs on either welds or brazes on any of the 326 tube-to-tube sheet joints and gives every indication of being able to fulfill its purpose in the reactor system.
Many thanks are due the Y-12 Shop personnel for their heartening cooperation and personal interest throughout both the developmental and construction stages of this project.
